title: Crooked Doghouse
summary: A storybook-crooked doghouse — the tilted, off-kilter look comes from four genuinely different truss angles, not from sloppy building.
description:
The whimsical "crooked house" look is deceptive: it takes MORE precision to build than a square one, not less. Every truss is a different, deliberate compound angle — 30°, 40°, 10° combinations cut long-point-to-long-point — and if you're sloppy instead of precise, you don't get charmingly crooked, you get a roof that doesn't close up right. Cut each truss exactly to its stated angle and the crookedness comes out looking designed, because it is.

Build all four trusses before you touch the floor panel, and dry-fit the whole roofline before you commit to gluing anything — the front and back overhang trusses have to land the same way the main trusses do, or the roof plywood won't lie flat. The curved gable shapes cut into the two large plywood panels are the other place to slow down: trace them from the plan's pattern, don't eyeball them, since those two panels are visible from the street on both sides of the house.

tools.note: A compound miter saw for the angled truss members — several of these cuts are off-square on both ends and not parallel to each other.
For breaking down the plywood panels.
For the curved gable shapes cut into the two large front/back plywood panels.

materials.name: Exterior plywood, 1/2", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: 2-1/2 sheets called for; buy 3 to allow for cutting waste on the angled panels.

materials.name: Wood glue, exterior/waterproof
materials.note: This lives outside — use a waterproof formula, not standard interior glue.

cutList.note: Top end cut at 45° off square, long-point measurement.
Top end cut at 45° off square, long-point measurement.
Both ends cut at 10° off square, NOT parallel, long-point measurement.
Curved gable shapes cut from these — trace the pattern, don't eyeball it.
Both ends 10° off square, long-point, NOT parallel.
One end of each cut at 5° off square, long-point.
One side 48" x 36", the other 48" x 32" — the crooked roofline doesn't match side to side.

steps.title: Cut and label every part
steps.body: Cut and label all the truss members before assembling anything — four trusses in several angle combinations are impossible to sort out later from a pile of look-alike sticks. From the 2x2 cut six top members 34 1/2" and six 30 1/2", each with its top end at 45° off square, long-point measurement; four bottom chords 29" with both ends at 10° off square and NOT parallel; four braces 23 3/4" with 30°/10° compound ends; four braces 21 3/8" with 40°/10° compound ends; and two ridge supports 48". Those twenty-six pieces pack into nine 8 ft boards, so the eleven listed leave two spare — cut a practice angle on a spare before touching the real stock. From the 1x3 cut two side trim 37", two straight front and back trim 29 3/4" (10° both ends, not parallel), two angled trim 23", two angled trim 25 1/4", one door trim top 25 3/4", and two door trim sides 18 1/2" with one end at 5°. From the 1x2 cut two lower side trim 18 3/4" and two upper 21 1/2". Break the plywood down last: the floor 29" x 39", the lower side panel 39" x 19 1/4", the upper 39" x 22", and the two gable panels 42 3/8" x 40 3/8". Leave the roof panels until the roofline is standing — the cut list gives both as 36" x 48" while its own note says one is 48" x 32", so that pair has to be measured rather than trusted.

steps.title: Build the four trusses
steps.body: Build each truss flat on the floor: the 34 1/2" and 30 1/2" top members meeting at the peak on their 45° ends, the 29" bottom chord across the base with its two non-parallel 10° ends, and one 23 3/4" and one 21 3/8" brace between them. The long and short top members are what throw the ridge off centre, so keep track of which side each goes on and build all four trusses the same way round. Predrill every end before driving a screw — 2x2 splits at the end grain, and a split member on a compound-angle joint cannot be pulled tight afterwards. Glue every joint with the waterproof glue and fasten with countersunk 2 1/2" screws. Lay the finished trusses on one another and record how yours actually differ; the description calls each one unique, but the cut list gives all four the same chord and brace lengths, so what varies is worth knowing before the panels go on.

steps.title: Mark and attach the floor
steps.body: Mark the four truss locations on the 29" x 39" floor panel, measuring every mark from the same end so the spacing cannot creep. The floor's 29" dimension matches the 29" bottom chords, so each truss sits square across the panel's width — that agreement is your check that you have the panel the right way round. Run a bead of waterproof glue where each truss lands, stand the trusses on their marks, and drive two or three 2" screws down through the plywood into each chord. Brace the assembly upright while the glue cures; a house this shape has almost no resistance to racking until the panels are on.

steps.title: Attach the end panels
steps.body: Trace the curved gable shapes from the plan's pattern onto the two 42 3/8" x 40 3/8" panels rather than eyeballing them — these are the faces of the house and any wobble in the curve is visible from the street — then cut them with the jigsaw, supporting the offcut so it cannot tear as it drops away. Cut the door opening in the front panel now, while the panel is still flat and reachable from both sides. Set each panel against its end truss, glue every bearing edge with the waterproof glue, and fasten with 2" screws into the truss members.

steps.title: Add the side plywood
steps.body: Attach the 39" x 19 1/4" lower side panel and the 39" x 22" upper panel with 2" screws and waterproof glue, lower panel first so the upper laps over it and sheds water outward rather than into the seam. Both are 39" long, matching the floor's 39" dimension, so their ends should land flush with the front and back panels. Screw into every truss the panel crosses, not just the ends.

steps.title: Trim the sides
steps.body: Trim out the sides using 1" finish nails and glue. The stated measurements run to the center of each stud, which makes for easier, more accurate nailing than measuring to a board edge.

steps.title: Trim the front and back
steps.body: Trim the front and back the same way, fitting the 29 3/4" straight pieces first and then the 23" and 25 1/4" angled pieces up the gable. Every one of these is a long-point measurement on a compound-angled end, so hold each piece against the actual panel and mark the cut there rather than trusting the tape — this is exactly where a sloppy cut turns charmingly crooked into actually does not fit. Glue with the waterproof formula and pin with 1" finish nails.

steps.title: Trim the front door opening
steps.body: Trim out the front door opening with glue and 1" finish nails.

steps.title: Add the overhang trusses
steps.body: Attach the front and back overhang trusses so they land the same way the main trusses do — if they sit even slightly out of plane, the roof plywood will not lie flat across them. Join the tops with screws and waterproof glue, and dry-fit the whole roofline before committing any fastener. Add the two 48" ridge supports between the trusses; they are not strictly required, but on a roof this shape they are cheap insurance and they give the roof panels something to land on between rafters.

steps.title: Add the roof
steps.body: Fit the two roof panels last, dry-laid and held with spring clamps before any fastener goes in. Because the truss angles differ side to side the two panels are not the same size, and the plan disagrees with itself about how: the cut list gives both as 36" x 48" while its own note says one is 48" x 32". Measure your assembled roofline and cut both panels to what you actually have. Run waterproof glue along every bearing edge and drive 2" screws down into the top members and ridge supports, spaced evenly along each rafter. Leave the panels slightly proud at the eaves rather than short — an overhang throws water away from the walls, a flush edge runs it straight down them.

images.alt: Whimsical crooked-style doghouse with an angled roofline and off-kilter trusses
